original_abstract:
A three-dimensional network solid, (NBu4)2FeIII2(dhbq)3, composed of iron(III) centers and paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers, is found to exhibit an electrical conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm at 298 K. This value represents one of the highest conductivities reported for a metal-organic framework (MOF) to date. Detailed characterization using spectroscopic techniques, variable-temperature conductivity, magnetic susceptibility, and slow-scan cyclic voltammetry reveals that the electronic transport is driven by organic mixed-valency within the ligand scaffold. Notably, UV-Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra provide the first evidence of Robin-Day Class II/III mixed valency in a MOF. 

Stoichiometric control over the ligand redox states was achieved through chemical reduction to produce the related framework Na0.9(NBu4)1.8FeIII2(dhbq)3. A comparative study between the parent and reduced materials shows that both electronic conductivity and magnetic ordering temperatures are governed by the specific ratio of the ligand redox states. The parent material exhibits ferrimagnetic ordering at low temperatures, with magnetic interactions persisting well above the transition point. These findings establish transition metal-semiquinoid MOFs as a versatile platform for achieving and tuning long-range electronic communication and high-temperature magnetic coupling in porous solids.
humanized_abstract:
(NBu4)2FeIII2(dhbq)3 consists of a three-dimensional network solid derived from iron(III) and paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers. As-synthesized materials had an electrical conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm at 298 K—the highest reported MOF conductivity. Studies involving 1H NMR, 13C NMR, variable-temperature conductivity, susceptibility, and slow-scan cyclic voltammetry indicate that the primary electronic transport mechanism within the material involves the organic mixed-valency found within the ligand framework. This study provides the first reported UV-Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra of Robin-Day Class II/III mixed valency within a MOF.

Stoichiometric control of the ligand redox states is achieved by chemical reduction to give the reduced framework material Na0.9(NBu4)1.8FeIII2(dhbq)3. A comparison of the physical properties of the parent material and the reduced material indicates that the conductivity and magnetic ordering temperature can be varied over wide ranges by simply changing the ligand redox state ratio. The parent material shows ferrimagnetic ordering at low temperature with magnetic interactions remaining active well above the ordering temperature. Therefore, transition metal–semiquinoid MOFs represent a unique class of porous materials that can afford tunable long-range electronic and high-temperature magnetic coupling.
